SUBJECT: U.S. Defense & Space Review
The time has come for a serious review of our Defense & Space
position for three reasons: (1) during the broader review of
national strategy last Spring, agencies were not prepared to step
up to the issues; (2) the Soviet position on linkage presented at
the Wyoming Ministerial has brought Defense & Space issues to
center stage, and, (3) Ambassadors Cooper and Burt have sent
backchannel messages to Secretaries Cheney and Baker respectively
suggesting ways to modify our position.
Our policy options are sufficiently detailed that they require
staffing before they can usefully be engaged by senior policy
makers. The paper at Tab I is an example of what such an options
paper might look like. But to conduct a successful review, we
need to have top-down guidance from your colleagues to their
staffs to take the review seriously.
The top-down guidance can be obtained in two ways. You might have
an informal discussion with Secretaries Baker and Cheney, Judge
Webster and General Powell that simply concentrates on the
importance of the review and the need to make it a thorough and
candid discussion of options. Or, you might use the paper at Tab
II, to focus such a discussion and provide more specific guidance
to the bureaucracy as to what is and is not under consideration.
This latter approach might be particularly useful should the
President wish to see options for big proposals (e.g. dropping
heretofore U.S. insistence on a right to deploy SDI, perhaps in
exchange for a ban on MIRV'ed ICBMs or some other large
objective.)
The advantage of the paper at Tab II is that it could serve as
the basis for more concrete guidance and therefore a more
productive review. The disadvantage is that it may not be
possible to provide such guidance.
Regardless of your decision on the paper at Tab II, we also need
commitments for your colleagues that the review will be handled

Purpose. To review U.S. Defense & Space positions in

U.S. Position. In returning to the Defense & Space talks
in June, the U.S. negotiating group was instructed to
pursue the following objectives:
o to preserve the right to pursue the SDI program
within the obligations of the ABM Treaty in
order to determine the technical feasibility
of advanced strategic defenses;
o to discuss with the Soviet Union a cooperative
transition to a strategic regime based on
effective defenses; and,
o to make explicit the right to deploy effective
strategic ballistic missile defenses after a
non-withdrawal period. (&)
The means by which the negotiating group was to pursue
these objectives were several fold:
o to preserve U.S. rights to conduct SDI testing,
while countering Soviet arguments that permitted
and prohibited activities under the ABM Treaty
must be agreed, the U.S. offered a unilateral
assurance that we would only conduct ABM tests
from designated test satellites, and that the
number of these platforms would be no greater
than fifteen. Furthermore, the U.S. proposed
that each side undertake to notify the other of
launches of ABM test satellites and ABM tests in
. orbit. (#4
o to promote stability during a transition, the
U.S. offered a protocol to a Defense & Space
Treaty, including reciprocal briefings and
visits to strategic defense sites. We also
proposed on-going discussions on strategic
stability to further a stable transition. (34
o to make explicit a right to deploy strategic
defenses after a non-withdrawal period, the U.S.
draft Defense & Space Treaty built on the
Washington Summit Joint Statement, in which
Gorbachev agreed that after a non-withdrawal
period, each side would be free to pursue its
own course of action. (8%)

The U.S. Defense & Space negotiating group returned to
Geneva for Round XII instructed to: probe the Soviets for
details of their new approach on Defense & Space issues;
continue discussions on predictability measures; and, seek
to determine if the Soviets have new proposals on
activities under the ABM Treaty. If asked about whether
the U.S. Treaty remains on the table, the negotiating
group was instructed to say that it is. Moreover, the
U.S. team was instructed to express the continued U.S.
commitment to evaluate the feasibility of effective
strategic defenses, and to deploy them when ready and to
oppose linkage or preconditions to START in any form.
Finally, the U.S. negotiating group was authorized to draw
on Secretary Baker's talking points from the ministerial
meeting, and did so, making the points that the U.S. seeks
a separate Defense & Space Treaty, embodying a commitment
to discussions of strategic stability lasting not more
than three years, at the end of which both sides would be
free to decide their course of action on strategic
defenses. (8)
Soviet Position. In June, the Soviets returned to the
Defense & Space talks with little changed. They continued
to link completion of a START Treaty to a U.S. commitment
to adhere to a narrow definition of the ABM Treaty. They
sought to use the U.S.-proposed predictability measures as
a means of verifying compliance with this standard. And,
they continued to refuse to work a joint draft Defense &
Space Treaty although such work had been agreed to at the
Washington Summit and subsequent ministerial meetings.
One new development was their hint that they would seek to
discuss ASAT limitations in Round XII. (8)
At the Wyoming ministerial, the Soviets announced that
they were dropping their requirement for a U.S. commitment
not to withdraw from the ABM Treaty as a pre-condition for
START. Both sides characterized this as delinkage. The
Soviets made clear however, that they claim a right to
withdraw from a START Treaty if they determine that the
U.S. has gone beyond the ABM Treaty as they define it.
V4 Thus, the Soviets have moved from linking their D&S
- position to the paper of a START Treaty to the reductions
following a START Treaty. (8)
The Soviets also said that they would have proposals for
resolving permitted and prohibited activities under the
ABM Treaty in a protocol to the ABM Treaty, which would
also include predictability measures. They have hinted
that the U.S. would find these proposals more acceptable
than the 1987 "list" proposal. Most recently they
proposed that the sides sign up to the narrow
interpretation of the ABM Treaty, citing the "Brown
Statement" on the distinction between research and
development. (83)

The U.S. draft Defense & Space Treaty calls for an
explicit right to deploy defenses after the non-withdrawal
period. The rationale for this position was two-fold:
first, it was a quid pro quo for the Soviet demand of a
non-withdrawal period; second, it furthered the U.S.
objective of facilitating deployment of strategic defenses
should they prove feasible. The position was crafted to
diminish political difficulties that would be inherent in
withdrawing from the ABM Treaty. (8)
The Soviet move at Wyoming probably reflects their
judgment that they overestimated our ability to withdraw
from the ABM Treaty. They were willing to drop their
demand for a non-withdrawal commitment confident that they
still explicitly held hostage START reductions to U.S.
adherence to the ABM Treaty as they define it. They were
particularly willing to make this move inasmuch as it
enabled them to evade Gorbachev's agreement at the
Washington Summit that after a non-withdrawal period "both
sides would be free to pursue their own course of action"
with respect to strategic defenses. At the time,
Secretary Carlucci confirmed with Shevardnadze that this
meant deployment. The U.S. can respond to the Soviet move
at Wyoming in several ways.

Issue
Should the U.S. modify its current positions on (a) its rights to
develop and teSt SDI under the ABM Treaty, and/or (b) its right
to deploy SDI?
Background
The Soviets say they have delinked START and Defense and Space in
order to facilitate early agreement on START. At the same time,
they have made clear that they reserve the right to withdraw from
the START Treaty if the U.S. goes outside the boundaries of the
ABM Treaty as the Soviets define those boundaries. These moves
have occurred against a backdrop of continuing erosion of
Congressional budgetary support for SDI. They also have spawned
public reactions from Sen. Nunn and Congressman Aspin (privately
echoed by Sen. Lugar) that a START agreement which is based on a
U.S.-Soviet "agreement to disagree" about the terms of the ABM
Treaty would get a cold reception on the Hill.
The key U.S. objectives in the Defense and Space Talks have been:
ds Broad interpretation of the ABM Treaty. To secure
Soviet acknowledgement of (or acquiescence in) our
right to conduct SDI research, development, and
testing under the broad interpretation of the ABM
Treaty; and
2. Right to deploy. To be able to deploy SDI
without the need to abrogate the ABM Treaty
and to secure Soviet agreement to (or
acquiescence in) that course of action.
In the view of some, the shift in the Soviet position provides
either the occasion or requirement for the U.S. to review one or
both of these objectives and/or our strategies for achieving
them. Such a review will require top-down direction to be
effective. The decision on how to proceed with be affected in
part by judgments about the prospects for realizing our two
objectives, and by the likely impact of the alternatives on SDI,
on the resolution of outstanding issues in START, and on
Congressional support for SDI and strategic force modernization.
Discussion
Visible and concrete progress on START could help sustain
Congressional support for both offensive force modernization and
SDI (the latter by demonstrating that SDI is not an "obstacle" to
a START agreement). Depending on whatever is agreed with the
Soviets about terms of the ABM Treaty, the result could provide
more latitude for SDI testing than Congress might permit in the
absence of such an agreement. Conversely, progress on SDI could

be hampered, either by the imposition of new limits on SDIrelated activities, or by the erosion of political and budgetary
support for the program. Moreover, if progress on START is seen
to have come at the expense of SDI, SDI supporters may be
unwilling to work for START ratification.
We can respond either tactically or strategically to the Soviet
moves. Tactical questions include whether to engage the Soviets
on their proposal to discuss "clarifications" of the ABM Treaty
that would define what SDI testing would be allowed and
forbidden, whether to continue to press the Soviets to
acknowledge a U.S. right to deploy SDI after an agreed period,
and how to engage the Soviet proposal for a mutual understanding
about ABM developments that would allow a side to withdraw from
the START Treaty. In principle, we could engage the Soviets on
issues such as these for some time whether or not we modify our
two core objectives, provided U.S.-Soviet agreement on them was
not deemed essential to our goals for START or SDI.
The strategic questions turn on whether and, if so, when we
should modify either or both of our D&S objectives.
Specifically, should we:
se Be prepared to seek agreement with the Soviets on
"clarifications" of the ABM Treaty that would permit
some SDI activities that go beyond the "narrow"
interpretation, but would prohibit some SDI activities
that would be accommodated under the "broad"
interpretation; or should we continue to press for
Soviet acceptance of our rights under the "broad"
interpretation?
2. Be prepared to defer resolution of our right to deploy
SDI after an agreed period; or should we continue to
press for Soviet acceptance of our right to deploy
without either requiring us to abrogate the ABM Treaty
or agreeing to Soviet abrogation of the START Treaty;
or should we be willing to put the possibility of
delaying SDI deployment "on the table" as a trade for a
Major Soviet concession in START (e.g., agreement to a
deMIRVing initiative).
Decisions on whether to address either or both strategic
questions now rest essentially on political judgments that must
be made at senior levels. Following these decisions, the
interagency process can develop the appropriate options and
analysis for senior level review.

almost come to seem the norm. Many of Gorbachevs unexpected moves have
stemmed from the urgency with which he is pursuing domestic change and his drive
to manage rather than respond to events. He has demonstrated that he sees his

deteriorating situation at home and unprecedented turmoil next door in Eastern
Europe. while paradoxically enjoying his strangest position ever within the Soviet
leadership. These circumstances suggest he will take more bold initiatives sooner
rather than Jater.!)

Setting the Stage: Changes in Style and Tactics
In his December meeting with the President, Gorbachev might deliberately abandon
his normally confident and persuasive demeanor in favor of a less confident or a more
confrontational stance. The main cause would be the deteriorating Soviet internal
situation and his urgent need for rapid progress on foreign policy issues that he sees as
directly affecting his domestic agenda. Gorbachev hopes to frame next fall's Party
Congress with concrete successes, especially in arms control. PUB
Any new tack could surface before rather than during the meeting with the President.
In the runup to the meeting, particularly while Gorbachev is in Italy, he could make a
series of public statements that would be intended to raise expectations and to set the
agenda for the talks. He might portray the meeting as a full "summit," hoping that the
United States would feel greater pressure to conclude some significant agreemer: it the
meeting, or at least to agree To Joint TNGUaGE INS "communique that laid out the goals
for future negotiations and put some time limits on reaching some of the most significant
agreements. ese
Gorbachev as Supplicant. The Soviet leader could come to the December meeting
with his hat in his hand. He would stress his own beleagured political position and urge
the President to take more concrete economic and political steps to support perestroyka.
Increased US support, he would argue, would support the position of reformers within the

SUBJECT: Soviet Summit Objectives
Gorbachev most likely will play his December 2-3 meeting with the
President as a full-fledged summit with primary emphasis on START
with a possible surprise offer and with some linkage to U.S.
naval reductions. He probably will not want to dwell much on
Eastern Europe or on regional problems.
On October 23, Shevardnadze told the Supreme Soviet: "Ahead lies
a new Soviet-American summit meeting" at which "an agreement on
strategic offensive arms" would, in effect, be "first and
foremost" on the agenda. He was clearly referring to the
December 2-3 meeting, of which he was already privy. Later in a
TASS interview, Shevardnadze stated: "I am confident that despite
its unofficial character, the Mediterranean meeting will be of
milestone importance. It will largely determine the importance
and substance of the official summit talks to be held late in
spring-early in summer next year." (Pravda, November 6, 1989).
On November 2, TANJUG quoted from an (Italian) ADNCRONOS-NEWS
interview with Shevardnadze in which he reportedly said that the
upcoming Mediterranean summit could "serve to lay the foundations
for an agreement on a 50 percent reduction of strategic arsenals
of the two big powers."
In raising START, Gorbachev obviously will further insist that
Soviet adherence to START reductions will depend on strict U.S.
compliance with the ABM Treaty. (Continued Soviet concern about
SDI is well reflected in Lt. General V. Starodubov's article in
Krasnaya Zvezda of October 24, 1989). In addition, Gorbachev may
well come up with a dramatic (at least in a PR sense) proposal on
START. (I trust our arms control people are conjuring up all
possible scenarios for such a Soviet surprise.)
It also seems likely that Gorbachev will exploit the naval
setting of the summit to press again for naval reductions and
attendant confidence building measures. A substantial reduction
in the U.S. Navy is a principal Soviet arms reduction goal. This
promotes the broad Soviet objectives of decoupling the U.S. from
Europe, of reducing the U.S. SLBM and SLCM threat to the USSR and

SUBJECT: Possible Initiatives in the Context of Malta
Following on your Wednesday breakfast discussion with Secretaries
Baker and Cheney, we met this morning with Reg Bartholomew, Paul
Wolfowitz, and Howard Graves in a principals-only "little five"
meeting. We described our task as identifying and assessing
various ways to get something from the Soviets for the defense
budget cuts we probably will be making in any event. We stressed
the obvious sensitivity of the enterprise, that there would be
zero tolerance for leaks, and that the information had to be
strickly limited to the people in room and their bosses. In that
spirit, no papers were circulated in advance or carried away from
the meeting.
The session was confined to talking through various
possibilities, with the two of us undertaking to write an issues
paper based on the discussion. That paper is attached. It has
not been shared with anyone. We plan to reconvene the group
tomorrow (Saturday) afternoon to go over it--after Wolfowitz and
Graves return from Camp David--making whatever changes are
necessary or desirable. After that, we will give you the revised
version to work with your counterparts and the President in the
run up to Malta.
One complication is that Cheney and Powell are scheduled to leave
for Europe Saturday evening, returning next Thursday. Wolfowitz
and Graves may be designated as their stand-ins, but you should
confirm this on the margins of your Camp David meeting tomorrow
morning.
Reading between the lines in today's meeting, it appears as
though Cheney and Powell may resist all efforts to advance any
concrete ideas or initiatives at Malta. They are inately
suspicious of any such approach, worry about the appearance or
reality of "Reykjavik II," and argue that we are running serious
risks of allowing Malta to force decisions about the defense
program and budget that have yet to be made. Based on a report
to him about out meeting this morning, Cheney is said to believe
that our effort to assess possible initiatives is a "bad idea."

SECREE
CHANGES IN THE DEFENSE PROGRAM AND
POSSIBLE INITIATIVES IN THE CONTEXT OF MALTA
The U.S. defense program probably will undergo a significant
restructuring and substantial reductions over the next few years.
This paper assesses alternative ways in which to portray certain
of these prospective defense program changes in the context of
the President's meeting with Gorbachev at Malta.
Whatever we do in this regard will bear directly on Allied
interests and their concerns that Malta might be a "Reykjavik
II." We face three broad choices about how to accommodate these
allied sensitivities.
(a) Consult with the Allies prior to Malta and shape an
approach to Gorbachev in light of those consultations.
(b) Sound out Gorbachev at Malta about various initiatives
and report to Allies on results at the December 4 NAC
in Brussels.
(c) Take a broad, philosophical approach at Malta that does
not result in any specific commitments or undertakings.
Each of these alternatives has significant disadvantages:
(a) Consulting with the Allies prior to Malta would risk
leaks, constrain the President's flexibility, and
aggravate fears that the meeting had become recast into
a Summit-like negotiation.
(b) Trying out ideas on Gorbachev without prior Allied
consultations could seem to the Allies as though they
had been misled about the purpose of Malta, that the
superpowers had cut deals over their heads, and that
they were being informed of a fait accompli.
(c) Taking a broad, philosophical approach could leave
Gorbachev uncertain about what we expected of him and
what we would be prepared to do in return. It could
seem as though we were setting high standards for
Soviet conduct, but subsequently be seen to take
actions unilaterally (because of budget pressures)
before those standards had been met.

CFE
Overview
The purpose here is to examine whether we can use CFE to try to
get something from the Soviets for cuts in U.S. force structure
that we will be forced to make anyway because of the budget
situation, and at the same time to accelerate the conclusion of a
CFE agreement. (DOD budget plans could require the withdrawal of
30,000 troops from Europe in FY 91 even if there is no CFE
agreement, and a cut of U.S. ground and air forces in Europe by
perhaps 150,000 by FY 94.) The options examined below would:
(1) drop aircraft and helicopters from CFE I and seek to persuade
Moscow to agree quickly, perhaps by April 1990, to a Treaty that
covers only tanks, armored troop carriers and artillery. This
would allow us to make major savings in the period ahead by both
rapidly implementing the 30,000 man European cut, and more
importantly, by reducing CONUS forces in anticipation of the very
large Soviet equipment reductions embodied in such a CFE I
Treaty. We would assure the Soviets that we would begin CFE II
expeditiously in order to respond to their expressed concerns
about Western aircraft and helicopters; and/or
(2) offer to lower the ceiling on U.S. and Soviet troops from
275,000 to 200,000 in CFE I, or alternatively and more to
Moscow's liking, promise now to do so as a subceiling in CFE II,
while at the same time meeting Soviet concerns by NATO agreeing
now to broaden the manpower ceiling in CFE II to cover all
indigenous and stationed troops in the Central Region.
The Soviets will find it hard to drop aircraft and helicopters
from CFE I without any concessions from our side, although their
budget problems might induce a change in their position. Winning
Allied support for any new CFE initiative would require
extraordinary efforts at the political level to duplicate the
exceptional consensus achieved at the NATO Summit in May.
Finally, getting a CFE agreement signed quickly will trigger soon
thereafter the beginning of SNF negotiations well before the West
German elections at the end of 1990, a fact that at a minimum
will deeply concern Mrs. Thatcher. (Imagine Helmut Kohl's
reaction if Gorbachev proposed a Third SNF Zero at the height of
the West German election campaign.)
Current Situation in Vienna
Although East and West are still struggling over counting rules
for tanks and armored troop carriers, there are no fundamental
reasons why these categories cannot soon join artillery in the
list of agreed definitions. Aircraft and helicopters present a

SECREP 3
different picture. Unless the East bends soon to the Western
position or the West makes concessions, the two sides must defer
these categories beyond CFE I if they are to gain quick
agreement.
Defer the Aircraft Category Until CFE IT
Paradoxically, both the U.S. and the Soviet Union expect to
benefit militarily from inclusion of combat aircraft in CFE
reductions. From the U.S. perspective, two factors are
paramount.
-- First, the Warsaw Pact has many more combat aircraft that
does NATO (over 14,000 to about 6700) and stands to take much
greater cuts under the current Western proposal (common ceiling
of 5,700). These cuts would tip the air balance decidedly toward
the West, given our qualitative edge.
= Second, NATO air forces are planning to reduce their
aircraft inventories by about 15% unilaterally in any event, for
budgetary reasons. So if we drop aircraft out of our CFE I
proposal, the West would make these cuts without necessarily
getting anything from the East in exchange.
Nevertheless, the Soviet attitude toward deferring this category
could well be negative, though perhaps somewhat ambivalent. Like
us, they would favor moves that would simplify the CFE I
negotiation and improve chances for an early agreement, not least
because an SNF negotiation would follow close behind. But
inclusion of aircraft was an Eastern proposal that the West had
long opposed, until we changed our position in May.
To some extent, the Soviets are now the prisoners of their own
rhetoric. For more than three years, in a determined effort to
portray the conventional military situation in Europe as
balanced, they have argued that Western advantages in aircraft
and helicopters offset admitted Eastern superiority in ground
combat items like tanks. This argument was based, in part, ona
genuine Soviet concern about the capacity of superior Western
aircraft to influence results on the battlefield. But the
Eastern fear never had a firm basis in actual aircraft numbers.
Moscow this spring distorted counting rules in tabling their
proposal in Vienna on aircraft so that the West would appear to
have more of the "tactical strike" aircraft that the East wanted
to cut. Soviet CFE representations are now putting forward
compromise ideas that would entail much larger Pact aircraft
cuts. Nevertheless, for both military and public diplomacy
reasons, the Soviets -- even at the top levels -- could find it
difficult to accept a CFE I agreement that left Western air
capabilities wholly unconstrained. With our continuing rejection

SBERET 4
of naval arms control, making NATO air forces immune from
immediate cuts as well could seem, from the Soviet perspective,
to mean that conventional arms control only emasculated Soviet
strength while ignoring Western advantages.
Our allies' views would be more mixed. The British and the
French never liked the idea of including aircraft in CFE I, at
least in part because of their dual use of conventional combat
aircraft as nuclear delivery systems. The Germans, on the other
hand, will accept any idea, whether to include aircraft or
exclude them, that will accelerate conclusion of a CFE I
agreement and the beginning of SNF negotiations. Since the NATO
Summit, however, all of our allies have become more aware, and
more interested, in the apparent prospect of asymmetrical Eastern
reductions in combat aircraft.
Defer the Helicopter Cagetory Until CFE IT
The best, and fastest, way to overcome current disagreements on
helicopters would be to drop the category from CFE I. Both sides
now agree, in principle, on an overall ceiling of 1900 combat
helicopters. They disagree on how to count combat helicopters.
The problem here is most difficult for NATO because it now seems
that sticking to the 1900 figure could force NATO to accept deep
(30% or more) cuts, causing bitter intra-alliance disputes over
the allocation of these reductions. Few in NATO would object to
dropping this category.
The most important military disadvantage is that it would leave
an important Warsaw Pact capability unconstrained in CFE I, with
the possibility, in the absence of a CFE II treaty, of
competition in helicopters supplanting competition in ground
attack fixed-wing aircraft. On the other hand, the Warsaw Pact
would not start such a competition from a position of great
advantage. There are about 1,200 Pact attack helicopters and
about 900+ on the NATO side, and a number of NATO countries have
active procurement programs underway to modernize and expand
their helicopter strength.
The Soviets can be expected to object to dropping helicopters out
of CFE I. As in the case of aircraft, Moscow originally proposed
inclusion of this category, arguing that it was an area of NATO
strength which counterbalanced Pact ground force advantages.
Asking the Soviets to drop both aircraft and helicopters from CFE
I_with nothing in return from the West would be a very big pill
for them to swallow, undoing what they thought they had gained
from the President's May initiative.
Propose Deeper U.S.-Soviet Manpower Cuts

We currently propose U.S.-Soviet reductions so that each country
would have no more than 275,000 ground and air personnel
stationed on foreign soil in Europe. This implies a reduction of
30,000 U.S. troops and about 320,000 Soviets.
To move beyond these reductions to a U.S.-Soviet stationed
ceiling of, say, 200,000 would mean that the U.S. would probably
have to rethink its strategy for defending the inter-German
frontier in two ways: (1) reconsider how many U.S. units need to
be forward deployed in peacetime, given the declining threat of a
short warning attack, and the effect of this change on our
reinforcement requirements; and (2) redistribute the remaining
U.S. forces in the FRG, perhaps radically thinning out the corps
now deployed on the Bavarian-Czech border.
Ceilings lower than 200,000 would force the U.S. to alter the
fundamental nature of its commitment to NATO, and would almost
certainly invalidate the strategy of forward defense as it is
currently understood. Merely to have a trained U.S.
infrastructure in place on the Continent for rapid reinforcement
requires a substantial number of personnel. If these are
maintained, ceilings on U.S. forces in Europe of 150,000 or
100,000 would leave less and less room for forward deployed
active units.
The problem we would have with Moscow on this score in CFE I
might not only be with the effect of this 200,000 U.S.-Soviet
ceiling on Soviet interests in Eastern and Central Europe. The
ceiling would not include other stationed forces, or other
indigenous forces. The Bundeswehr would therefore be
unconstrained and well over 400,0000 in strength, a dark and
forboding shadow from the Soviet Union's past.
Other implications of a 200,000 U.S. ceiling in CFE I could be:
-- The allies may worry that we are leaving Europe. Many of
them, especially Mrs. Thatcher, believe our troops are there in
current circumstances in large part to help manage the Germans.
-- U.S. cuts could therefore seriously unsettle the center of
Europe at a particularly delicate time.
-- Larger U.S. and Soviet reductions in the near term could
trigger unilateral cuts across NATO which, along with the
increasingly difficulty of modernizing U.S. nuclear weapons on
the Continent, could badly weaken the credibility of Flexible
Response.
= Such an initiative in CFE I could altogether ruin the
President's May NATO Summit triumph, breaking apart the Allied

SEGREP 6
solidarity he cemented together.
In order to make this idea more palatable to the Allies, and more
interesting to the Soviets, one could propose now that these
additional U.S. troop reductions be made in a rapidly negotiated
CFE II as part of a broader manpower ceiling on all forces in
Central Europe.
Include all Manpower in the Central Region
Another way to implement steeper U.S. troop cuts might be in
connection with a general manpower ceiling in CFE II for the
Central Region, including both indigenous and stationed forces.
The ceiling could include a subceiling for a U.S. and Soviet
manpower. This approach would resemble the old NATO position in
MBFR. Grinevsky floated just such an idea with regard to CFE I
in his recent meeting with Woolsey, suggesting a 275,000 U.S.-
Soviet subceiling and a 570,000 total for the Central Region
(which would include the FRG, the Benelux countries, and possibly
Denmark on the Western side and the GDR, Poland, Czechoslovakia,
and possibly Hungary in the East).
We could structure a proposal in CFE II for a ceiling on U.S. and
Soviet forces of 200,000, and a common manpower ceiling for each
side in the Central Region -- including Denmark and Hungary.
This would both allow our allies to participate in reductions
during CFE II, and meet Soviet concerns about the size of the
Bundeswehr.
The Soviets would consider inclusion of the Bundeswehr in NATO's
troop ceilings to be a major Western concession, and attach a
particular premium to this move given recent events in the GDR.
It is unclear whether this would be enough to convince Moscow to
go ahead with CFE I without aircraft and helicopters and to
accept a U.S.-Soviet manpower ceiling of 275,000.
Since the main impact of the additional ceiling would be the
inclusion of the Bundeswehr, it is noteworthy that the FRG
negotiators in Vienna praised Grinevsky's idea for an overall
Central European manpower ceiling last week in a private meeting
with Woolsey, saying this option would be best from the
standpoint of German internal politics and in light of planned
unilateral cuts in the Bundeswehr.
The West could explain that it had singled out the Central Region
for a manpower ceiling in CFE II in order to find a solution to
the disagreement with the East on how to limit Soviet manpower in
Eastern Europe, stressing the fact that the Soviet ceiling would
be even lower under this scheme. There are also the usual
arguments about the special salience of the Central Region in the

If we were able to resolve East-West differences on aircraft,

The toughest challenges would probably be within the Alliance.

Background
Approximately $800 million is included in the defense program
between now and FY 1994 for binary modernization. The cost to
the year 2000 is projected to be approximately $3 billion. The
need to reduce defense spending could lead to cuts--perhaps
substantial--in the binary modernization program. Separately,

Congress could impose sharp spending limitations on binary CW,
either in the context of overall cuts in the defense budget or
due to opposition to the modernization program itself. In either
event, the feasibility of modernizing the CW stockpile with

Bigeye air-delivered munition, could be in doubt.
The Soviets say they stopped producing CW in 1987, and challenged
us to cease CW production immediately. They have not, however,
responded to the President's proposal for bilaterial reductions
down to 20 percent of the current U.S. stockpile, and have
avoided engaging us in discussions about it. There is, of
course, great uncertainty about the size of the current Soviet CW

Possible Initiative
The U.S. could offer to end CW binary production before the
modernizaton program is scheduled to be completed in exchange for
Soviet endorsement of the President's CW initiative, viz.,
reductions to 2 percent of the current U.S. stockpile within
eight years after the chemical weapons convention enters into
force, preceded by bilateral reductions to 20 percent of current
U.S. levels. The U.S. offer to cease CW production could be cast
either in a multilateral or bilateral context:
(a) multilateral: In exchange for Soviet agreement to the
20 percent/2 percent approach, agree to cease CW
production as soon as the chemical weapons convention
enters into force (an act that would require Soviet
participation).
(b) bilateral: In exchange for Soviet agreement to reduce
to 20 percent of the U.S. stockpile, agree to cease CW
production as soon as a Memorandum of Understanding is
signed, preferably at the 1990 Summit.
A variant of the bilateral alternative would be to combine this
offer with a challenge to the Soviets to reduce our respective CW
stockpiles down to 10 percent of current U.S. levels, i..e, well
below the impact of the Congressionally-mandated unilateral
reductions but within the range of a near-term binary production
program.
Such an initiative could take advantage of possible unilateral
curtailment in our CW modernization program to help lock in
Soviet agreement to the President's CW initiative. It also might
help strengthen Congressional support for the program in the
interim. On the other hand, it could sacrifice CW modernization
that the budget and the Congress might otherwise allow. By

Background
Although specific decisions have not been made about the post-
START strategic force structure, several options are under
consideration as part of the review of the defense program.
These include downloading Minuteman III missiles to a 1-RV
configuration and retiring Minuteman II. In combination with
plans to redeploy Peacekeeper in the rail-garrison basing mode,
this option would result in the phase out of silo-based MIRVed
ICBMs.
Congress made relatively minor reductions in the budget requests
for rail-garrison Peacekeeper and SICBM in the FY 1990 budget
that was just passed. Future Congressional support for both
mobile ICBM programs remains uncertain.
The defense program currently includes plans to build one Trident
SSBN per year. Longer-term projections of this building rate
would yield a fleet of about 30 Trident submarines by the year
2000 (well above any plausible START-constrained force
structure). The long-lead items for the eighteenth Trident and
the core for the nineteenth Trident are included in the justpassed FY 1990 defense budget. Budget pressures could lead to a
reduced Trident building rate and/or a curtailed Trident fleet.
Another consideration as the defense program is restructured is
that START constraints will force tradeoffs between ICBM RVs and
SLBM RVs.
In the past, the Soviets have argued for the elimination of
nuclear weapons. More recently, they have suggested that the two

interim step to eliminating nuclear weapons entirely. Gorbachev
could make a proposal along these lines at Malta.
Possible Initiative
The U.S. could propose to:
(a) lower START limits from the current 6000 warheads by an

more expensive.
SICBM would be critical to the continued viability of the ICBM
leg of the Triad under this approach. It probably would be
desirable to accelerate its IOC, but it is doubtful that the
timetable could be appreciably shortened. If SICBM were not
deployed for whatever reason, this proposal would be seen by many
as tantamount to a U.S. decision to move to an "augmented dyad"
for nuclear deterrence. Phase-out of Peacekeeper missiles might
produce some near-term budget savings as a result of cancellation
of the rail-garrison rebasing program, but would result in the
elimination of our most modern and capable ICBM RVs. The
implications of such a cancellation for continued Congressional
support for SICBM are unclear.
Under this approach, we would not fund any new starts for Trident
submarines and probably would not spend some of the money in the
FY 1990 and FY 1991 budgets for long-lead items.
This proposal would have a much larger impact on the Soviets.
They could reject it amid angry charges of American
"grandstanding" at what was supposed to be an informal, no agenda
meeting at Malta. They could well counter with proposals to ban
SLCMs, or sharply limit or ban MIRVed SLBMs.
Variants of the proposal could limit the MIRV ban either to all
silo-based MIRVed missiles, or all mobile MIRVed missiles. A ban
on mobile MIRVs would permit the U.S. to retain Peacekeeper
missiles in silos (and allow the Soviet Union to retain its
SS-18s and SS-24s). It would reduce the costs of strategic force
modernization to the U.S., and provide a hedge against the
uncertain prospects for SICBM. It would require us to explain

SECRET il
how we reconcile our START emphasis on strategic stability with
an approach that sanctioned vulnerable MIRVs and banned
survivable MIRVs. It probably would be marginally more
acceptable to the Soviets than would a total ban on MIRVed ICBMs.
Assuming a force of 800 ICBM RVs, a ban on silo-based MIRVed
ICBMs would permit the U.S. to proceed with rebasing Peacekeeper,
and to deploy either 300 SICBMs or 300 1-RV Minuteman IIIs. The
Soviets would have to eliminate all of their SS-18s and silobased SS-24s. It would not mitigate, and could aggravate, the
verification and breakout risks posed by mobile MIRVs. The
Soviets probably would find a ban on silo-based MIRVs more
difficult to accept than a mobile MIRV ban unless they could be
persuaded that the phase-out of their silo-based force was in
their long term interest, or that U.S. concessions on other START
issues, combined with a rapid conclusion of a START agreement,
made it worthwhile.
Naval Force Reductions and Naval Arms Control
Background
Although we have made clear that we categorically reject the
idea, there is a widespread expectation that Gorbachev will
propose naval arms control in some form or other at Malta.
Budget pressures are likely to lead to some reductions in naval
forces. Options under consideration include early retirement of
two carriers, mothballing some or all of the battleships.
Escorts would be reduced proportionally.
Possible Initiative
The U.S. would continue to reject all efforts to engage us in
discussions or negotiations about naval arms control. We could
respond to such proposals, however, by observing that:
(a) U.S. requirements for naval forces depend upon threats
to our interests around the world.
(b) As those threats diminish, our requirements will be
adjusted accordingly.
(c) Reductions in Soviet military capabilities
particularly those that are well-suited for offensive
missions, are one of the things that would lead to
lessened threats and lowered requirements for naval
forces.

SBCRET 12
(d) Reductions in regional tensions and regional
instabilities likewise would permit smaller U.S.naval
forces. The Soviet Union could help us address such
regional problems.
Defense Spendin
Background
The U.S. defense budget has declined in real terms every year
since FY 1985. Even deeper reductions are in prospect. There
are some signs that trends in Soviet defense spending also may be
turning down. Because of the vast disparities in the relative
sizes of our two economies, a given level of Soviet defense
spending consumes a much larger percentage of their GNP than an
equivalent expenditure requires of ours.
Possible Initiative
The U.S. could challenge the Soviet Union to reduce our
respective "defense burdens," measured as a percentage of GNP, by
15-20 percent. For the U.S., this would reduce the amount of the
GNP devoted to defense by about 1 percent. For the Soviets, the
reduction in GNP spent on defense probably would be in excess of
3 percent.
ASAT
The implications of defense budget cuts, if any, for our ASAT
program are sufficiently uncertain that it does not seem
worthwhile to raise the subject in any way at Malta.

Defense and Space
-- At the 1987 Washington Summit, President Reagan and General
Secretary Gorbachev agreed to the phrase "research,
development and testing as required which are permitted
by the ABM Treaty as signed in 1972."
-- It is well known to the Soviet Union that the U.S...
understanding of this phrase is that the Parties agreed
that the ABM Treaty permits the testing of certain
ABM systems in space.
-- Recent statements by the Soviet side suggest that the
exercise of these existing rights under the ABM Treaty
would constitute a violation of this Treaty and grounds
for withdrawal from a future START Treaty.
-- This is simply not the case. The exercise of existing
rights to test ABM systems based on other physical principles
in space is not a violation of the ABM Treaty and, as
such, does not constitute grounds for the Soviet Union to
avoid its current or future Treaty obligations.
-- Since our meeting in Wyoming, Soviet attempts to "relink"
START with constraints on the SDI program have become an
obstacle to progress in negotiations and, if not dropped,
will obstruct ratification of a START agreement.
-- The United States proceeds towards a START agreement with
the understanding that Soviet compliance with a START
agreement will not be conditioned on the requirement that
the United States waive or forfeit rights under the
ABM Treaty.

I. PURPOSE
To discuss Congressional support for the defense budget,
especially the strategic modernization program and its
relationship to START.
II. BACKGROUND
This is an informal meeting held at the request of Senators
Nunn and Warner to express their concerns about the
prospects on the Hill for the strategic modernization
program. They will tell you that the "two-missile program"
will not survive without a major arms control initiative and
that B-2 and SDI are "in trouble." You will want to draw
them out on these subjects as well as on Congressional
support for troop levels in Europe and on the defense budget
as a whole.
Support for strategic modernization last year was very thin
and required the investment of considerable political
capital and time by you, Dick Cheney and our arms control
negotiators. The political climate this year is far less
supportive. We face a major attack on the defense budget,
the belief by some that the strategic threat is now
appreciably lessened, a growing clamor for payment of the
"peace dividend," and election year politics. Yet with
START in the balance, strategic modernization may be even
more important.
ICBM Modernization. Your FY 1991 defense budget calls for
$2.8 billion for the Peacekeeper ICBM, most of which is for
the redeployment of the missile from its silos to rail
garrison. It also includes $202 million for continued
development of the small mobile ICBM, the Midgetman. The
first trains with Peacekeeper would be deployed in 1992 and
the first Midgetman in 1997.
The two missiles have survived in Congress as a package.
The coalition that has supported both missiles was built
largely around political compromises to support one missile
in return for support for the other, and was never very

strong. That coalition is vanishing and there has not been
a majority who support either missile for a year or more.
Now Warner has said that we cannot afford both systems; Nunn
has called for a revision to START to ban mobile MIRVs and
said that we should delay the treaty if necessary to achieve
this; Aspin has said the US can no longer justify systems as
needed for bargaining leverage. These are the members who
led the fight to support the two missile program last year.
If both programs are cancelled there is a real possibility
that the START treaty would not be ratified since the
Soviets already have two deployed mobile systems and we will
have none. This problem will be compounded if the B-2
program is sharply curtailed and we are not able to take
full advantage of the bomber counting rule in START.
Nunn will push for a change to our START position to
explicitly ban mobile MIRVs. While I am attracted to this
idea, I am concerned that anything we do now will delay the
conclusion of START and potentially give the Soviets an
opening to charge that we are intentionally delaying the
treaty. I will have some specific ideas to discuss with you
soon. At this point, however, I note that there are no
quick or simple ways to change START to rebuild support on
the Hill for our ICBM modernization program.
B-2. Your budget proposal calls for $5.5 billion and the
authorization to produce five additional aircraft. Last
fall Congress reduced the plan from five aircraft to two and
added a number of testing and reporting requirements. This
year support for the aircraft is weakening further, and the
aircraft cost makes this an easy target (although about half
of the total program cost already has been spent).
The full program is for a buy of 132 aircraft and that is
what is presented in your budget. Dick Cheney has ordered a
full review of the program by March, after which he will
make his recommendations. Best guess now is that he will
call for a total buy of 75-85 aircraft instead of the 132
now planned.
Nunn and Warner should be generally supportive of the
program but will be concerned about the costs -- both
monetary and political. Both have supported the technology
but will not support the full 132 aircraft buy. Nunn
argues, I think correctly, that we have not done a good job
defining the mission for this aircraft. We do need the B-2
if we are to enter the next century with a penetrating
bomber. Numbers much below 75 do not make much sense to me
from either a financial or operational point of view.
Congressional support of the B-2 will be important, however,
for ratification of START. As you recall, bombers that do

Trident/D-5. The Trident submarine program is in good shape
on the Hill. This next year's budget funds the production

Next year, however, we will need to decide if we want to

SDI. The FY 1991 budget calls for $4.8 billion for SDI.
This level of funding is required to support large-scale

been eroding -- last year's initial request was for $5.6
billion, later revised in your budget to $4.9 billion and

the changes in the political environment are increasing

In the past both Nunn and Warner supported the program and
Nunn feels he carried the Administration's water during last
fall's Congressional budget action. Some on the Hill
question the Administration's support of SDI because we
acquiesced to last year's cut in the program. We anticipate
a substantial cut again this year unless you go to the mat
for it. Nunn and Warner will want to know whether you are
prepared to make an all out push including a veto threat if
necessary.
Nunn would likely be content with a program that focused on
long-term R&D, bypassing the near-term deployment option.
Warner may believe we are spending too much on near-term
technologies. Nevertheless, as you were told at Livermore,
Brilliant Pebbles accounts for only about three percent of
the SDI budget, despite its high profile. The overall
balance is about 50-50 near term and long term.
Troop levels in Europe. Soviet agreement to your 195,000
manpower initiative in Central Europe should have
strengthened Congressional support for maintaining this
level, but there will be calls to reduce further as the
Soviets inevitably pull out and German unification proceeds.
While you will want to hear Nunn and Warner's views on how
Congress will treat our military presence in Europe, you
should emphasize that their support is essential and that we
cannot now go lower without severe damage to the Alliance,
the future of Germany, and our relations with our strongest
allies.

briefly the overall level of the defense budget of $295
